title: Predicting the SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic IPOs — With Dick Costolo
summary: Alex Kantrowitz interviews Dick Costolo, ex-CEO of Twitter and managing partner at 01 Advisors, to discuss the anticipated IPOs of SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic. The discussion covers IPO timing, the differences between these companies, how capital will be deployed, and whether AI companies represent genuine platforms.
writeup: Big Technology Podcast host Alex Kantrowitz interviews Dick Costolo, former Twitter CEO who took the company public in 2013, about what SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic face as they approach the public markets. The conversation uses the Twitter IPO as a case study in how the narrative a company tells at its debut becomes the yardstick investors hold it to quarter after quarter, independent of underlying business performance. The through-line is that trillion-dollar AI companies heading into public markets carry expectations so extreme that even strong results can disappoint if the founding hype is outrun.
key_points: The narrative a company sets at IPO becomes a permanent benchmark: Twitter beat top-line revenue and EBITDA in multiple quarters but saw its stock fall whenever monthly active users missed consensus, because the company had publicly promised a path to one billion users and the market measured it against that promise every quarter.

Dick Costolo's core advice to first-time pre-IPO executives is to prepare the internal team for stock price moves of 15 to 20 percent in either direction with no corresponding business event, since the transition from private to public markets introduces daily price volatility that employees never experienced on the private side.

SpaceX's IPO is expected to launch with a very small float (a small percentage of total shares issued), which combined with enormous retail and institutional demand could push the day-one price to what Costolo calls an "insane number" that does not reflect the underlying business math and could correct sharply within days.

Elon Musk's sustained retail narrative, demonstrated with Tesla, gives SpaceX an unusual advantage: even when near-term numbers miss (63 robotaxis deployed in Austin versus an earlier estimate of one million), the market accepts a forward-looking "but here is where we are going" framing that most CEOs cannot sustain.

The SpaceX public narrative is expected to center on Starlink powering global internet connectivity, with a longer-term claim around orbital data centers that Costolo explicitly compares to Elon's 2013 prediction that full self-driving would arrive within a year, calling both claims very far from being true in the near term.
